turns.content: can you help me think through going back to school? I've been going back and forth on this for months and I need to just make a decision

Absolutely. Let's break it down so you can get to a decision instead of circling. What kind of program are you considering, and where are you starting from? Like what's your current education and work situation?

so I'm an RN, I have my BSN, and I've been working pediatric nursing for about 4 years. I'm looking at nurse practitioner programs because I think that's where I want to go long-term, I want to be able to do more for my patients and honestly the earning potential is a lot better too. DePaul has a really good DNP program with a pediatric NP track and I've been looking at it

Pediatric NP is a strong path, especially with 4 years of bedside peds experience — that clinical foundation matters a lot in NP programs and you'd be going in with real context for what you're learning.

DePaul's DNP program is well-regarded. A few things to map out:

1. **Program structure** — Is it full-time or part-time? Online, hybrid, in-person? This determines whether you can keep working.
2. **Timeline** — DNP programs are typically 3-4 years part-time. Are you OK with that commitment?
3. **Cost and funding** — Tuition, whether your employer offers tuition reimbursement, loan situation
4. **Work compatibility** — Can you keep your current job while in school?

Which of these is the biggest sticking point for you?

all of them honestly lol. ok so DePaul's program is hybrid, classes on weekends and some online which is why I was drawn to it because theoretically I could still work. but I work nights and the idea of working 3 twelve-hour night shifts AND going to class on weekends makes me want to cry just thinking about it

Yeah, that's the honest reality of it — working full-time nights plus a weekend hybrid program is brutally hard. Not impossible, plenty of nurses do it, but you should go in with eyes open about what that actually looks like week to week.

Let's map the math:
- 3 twelve-hour night shifts = 36 hours of work
- Weekend classes (probably one full Saturday or two half-days)
- Clinical hours once you're in the clinical phase of the program (these are usually daytime, which creates a schedule conflict with your sleep)
- Studying/coursework: probably 15-20 hours per week on top of everything

So realistically you'd need to either:

**Option A: Stay full-time, suffer for 3-4 years.** You'll be exhausted, your social life will shrink to almost nothing, but you finish with no income gap.

**Option B: Drop to part-time (2 shifts per week).** Financially harder, but survivable. Much more sustainable energy-wise. This is what a lot of nurses do.

**Option C: Save aggressively for a year, then go full-time student.** Fastest completion, but biggest financial risk.

Does your hospital do tuition reimbursement? That could shift the math significantly.

they do actually, Lurie has a tuition assistance program but I think it's capped at like $5,000 a year which honestly doesn't go that far for a DNP program. DePaul is not cheap. and I already have loans from my BSN that I'm still paying off so taking on more debt is terrifying

The debt anxiety is completely valid. Let me help you frame the numbers so it's less of a vague fear and more of a concrete calculation.

**The cost side:**
- DePaul's DNP is roughly $50,000-$60,000 total (you'd want to verify current numbers)
- Minus ~$5,000/year from Lurie x 3-4 years = $15,000-$20,000 offset
- Net new debt: roughly $35,000-$45,000 on top of your existing BSN loans

**The earnings side:**
- Pediatric NP salary in Chicago: roughly $95,000-$115,000 starting, more with experience
- RN salary (I'm guessing you're in the $65,000-$80,000 range with night diff?)
- That's a $25,000-$40,000 annual raise once you're practicing as an NP

So the additional debt could be recovered in roughly 1-2 years of NP salary, even accounting for loan payments. The ROI on BSN-to-DNP is genuinely one of the better ones in healthcare education.

**Other funding to look into:**
- HRSA Nurse Corps Scholarship — covers tuition and fees plus a monthly stipend in exchange for 2 years of service in an underserved area after graduation
- Illinois nursing scholarships (there are state-specific ones)
- Employer loan repayment — some hospitals will help repay your existing BSN loans if you commit to staying

Have you looked at any of these?

I didn't know about the HRSA thing that's actually really interesting because I would absolutely work in an underserved area, that's kind of the whole point for me. I grew up in a community where people couldn't afford to take their kids to the doctor and it messed me up honestly, I want to be accessible to families like mine

That motivation is going to carry you through the hard parts of the program. And HRSA Nurse Corps was basically designed for people with your exact goal — they fund people who want to practice in communities that don't have enough providers.

The scholarship is competitive but your profile is strong: you're already working in pediatrics, you have a clear vision for underserved care, and you come from the community you want to serve. That's a compelling application.

Application typically opens in the spring — you'd want to check the current cycle timeline on the HRSA website. Even if you don't get it the first year, there are also loan repayment programs through HRSA that could help with your existing BSN debt.

OK so let me ask the other big question: timeline. When are you thinking of starting? DePaul probably has cohorts starting in fall and maybe spring. Are you looking at this fall or giving yourself more time?

I was thinking maybe fall 2025 but the application deadline might have already passed I'm not sure, I've been procrastinating looking into it because it makes it feel real and real is scary. if I missed fall I guess spring 2026?

The procrastination-because-it-feels-real thing is incredibly common with big life decisions. But here's the thing — you've clearly already decided you want to do this. You've researched the program, you know why you want it, you have a vision for your career after. The decision is made. What's left is execution.

Here's what I'd suggest as immediate next steps:

**This week:**
1. Go to DePaul's DNP program page and check the application deadline for Fall 2025. If it's passed, check Spring 2026.
2. Look up the HRSA Nurse Corps Scholarship application timeline.
3. Call or email DePaul's admissions — they're usually happy to walk you through requirements and answer questions. Sometimes just talking to a human makes it less overwhelming.

**This month:**
4. Talk to your manager at Lurie about the tuition assistance process — what paperwork, when to apply, any conditions.
5. Look into whether going part-time is financially feasible. Run your budget with 2 shifts per week instead of 3-4. What's the gap and can you cover it with loans/savings?
6. Start gathering application materials: transcripts, letters of recommendation (your charge nurse, a physician you work with), personal statement.

**The personal statement:**
You basically just told me your personal statement. You grew up in a community without access, you've seen what happens when kids can't get care, you want to be the provider those families need. That's powerful and authentic. Don't overthink it.

Does breaking it into steps like this make it feel more manageable?

ok yes that's so much better than the giant terrifying blob it was in my head. and you're right I think I have decided, I'm just scared. my parents didn't go to college so figuring all this out feels like I'm making it up as I go which I guess I kind of am

You're not making it up — you're navigating a system that wasn't designed with first-generation students in mind, and that takes more effort because you don't have the inherited knowledge that some people do. But you already navigated it once for your BSN and you came out the other side. You know how to do hard things in unfamiliar territory.

A DNP is a doctorate. You'd be Dr. Vasquez. Let that sit for a second.

A few practical tips for the navigation part:
- DePaul likely has advisors specifically for working professionals in their nursing programs. Use them aggressively — that's what they're there for.
- Find someone in the program or a recent grad and talk to them. Nothing beats "what is this actually like" from someone who just did it. DePaul admissions can often connect you with current students.
- Your hospital probably has NPs who went through similar programs while working. Buy one of them coffee and ask how they did it.

You've got the motivation, the clinical experience, and a clear reason to do this. The logistics are solvable. Go check that application deadline this week.
Dr. Vasquez... ay dios mio my mom would literally cry. ok I'm doing this. I'm checking the deadline tonight. thank you for helping me organize my brain on this I've been spinning for months

Your mom should cry — that's a big deal.

Check the deadline tonight, and if it's still open for fall, don't let it sit. Start the application even if you just fill in the basic info. Momentum matters more than perfection at this stage.

You're going to be a great NP. Go make it happen.
